section.title: The Cheerful Giver
section.sectionRationale: Paul brings the appeal for the collection to its climax. He moves from the practical arrangements to the heart of the giver and the character of God. Using the image of sowing and reaping, he calls for glad, uncompelled generosity, and assures the Corinthians that God is able to supply everything they need so that they may abound in good works. The passage ends not with the gift but with the Giver, in a burst of praise for God's indescribable gift.
section.thesis: God loves a cheerful giver, and He is able to make all grace abound so that those who give generously will themselves be enriched in every way; for the God who supplies the seed multiplies the harvest, and the ministry of giving overflows in thanksgiving that rises to the God whose indescribable gift is Christ Himself.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the law of the harvest and the cheerful giver whom God loves; the God who supplies seed and multiplies righteousness so His people abound in every good work; and the overflow of thanksgiving to God, climaxing in praise for His indescribable gift.

verses.text: Remember this: Whoever sows sparingly will also reap sparingly, and whoever sows generously will also reap generously.
Each one should give what he has decided in his heart to give, not out of regret or compulsion. For God loves a {{hilaros|cheerful}} giver.
And God is able to make all grace abound to you, so that in all things, at all times, having all that you need, you will {{perisseuo|abound}} in every good work.
As it is written: “He has scattered abroad His gifts to the poor; His righteousness endures forever.”

units.label: God supplies the seed
units.note: enriched in every way to be generous

verses.text: Now He who supplies seed to the sower and bread for food will supply and multiply your store of seed and will increase the harvest of your righteousness.
You will be enriched in every way to be generous on every occasion, and through us your generosity will produce {{eucharistia|thanksgiving}} to God.
For this ministry of service is not only supplying the needs of the saints but is also overflowing in many expressions of thanksgiving to God.

Because of the proof this ministry provides, the saints will glorify God for your obedient confession of the gospel of Christ, and for the generosity of your contribution to them and to all the others.
And their prayers for you will express their affection for you, because of the surpassing grace God has given you.
Thanks be to God for His indescribable gift!

hilaros.gloss: Cheerful, glad, joyous; the source of the English word hilarious. Paul says that God loves a hilaros giver, one whose giving springs from a glad and willing heart rather than reluctance or coercion. The word points to a deep, joyful readiness that delights in the act of giving itself. God is not merely satisfied with the gift; He loves the giver whose generosity flows from joy.

perisseuo.gloss: To abound, to overflow, to have more than enough. Paul uses it to describe the abundance God supplies, not so that His people may hoard, but so that they may perisseuo, overflow, in every good work. The word carries the picture of a cup filled past the brim, a sufficiency from God that spills over into generosity toward others and finally into thanksgiving to God.

eucharistia.gloss: Thanksgiving, the giving of thanks, gratitude expressed. Paul says that the generosity of the Corinthians will produce eucharistia to God, and that the ministry overflows in many expressions of thanksgiving. The word shows that giving is not merely horizontal, meeting human need, but vertical, rising back to God as praise. Generosity completes its circuit only when it becomes worship in the mouths of those who are helped.

basic.context: Paul reaches the heart of his appeal for the collection. He sets out the law of the harvest, that sparing sowing brings a sparing crop and generous sowing a generous one, and he calls for giving that is glad and free, not grudging or forced, because God loves a cheerful giver. He then lifts the Corinthians' eyes to God, who supplies all they need, multiplies their harvest, and receives the thanksgiving that their generosity produces, ending in praise for His indescribable gift.
basic.hermeneutics: The sowing and reaping image must be read as gospel promise, not as a formula for getting rich. The harvest Paul has in view is a harvest of righteousness and thanksgiving to God, not guaranteed personal wealth. Read verses 6 to 11 in the light of verses 12 to 15, where the goal is that God is glorified and thanked.
basic.originalLanguages: God loves a hilaros giver, a cheerful, glad-hearted one, the word from which we get hilarious. He makes grace abound so that we perisseuo, overflow, in every good work. And generosity produces eucharistia, thanksgiving, to God, so that giving finally becomes worship.
basic.typology: The sowing and reaping of the field, and the God who gives seed to the sower and bread for food, draw on the whole biblical picture of harvest as a sign of God's provision. This points to Christ, the seed that fell into the ground and died to bear a great harvest, and to the indescribable gift in whom all God's blessings are given.
basic.christ: The passage ends with thanks to God for His indescribable gift, which is ultimately Christ Himself, God's own Son given for us. All Christian generosity is a response to that gift, a small imitation of the God who gave His best. Every act of giving is enfolded in the confession of the gospel of Christ that the saints glorify God for.
basic.god: God dominates these verses. He loves the cheerful giver, He is able to make all grace abound, He supplies and multiplies the seed, He increases the harvest of righteousness, and He receives the thanksgiving. The passage exalts a generous, all-sufficient God who gives so that His people may give, and who turns their generosity into worship of Himself.
basic.crossReferences: Proverbs 11 says the generous will be enriched. Luke 6 promises a good measure, pressed down and running over. Philippians 4 says God will supply every need according to His riches in Christ. John 3 declares that God so loved the world that He gave His only Son.
basic.oneStory: The Bible reveals a God who gives, supremely in the gift of His Son. The cheerful giver reflects that God, sowing generously because he has first received the indescribable gift. And the whole circuit of giving comes back to its source as thanksgiving, so that human generosity ends where it began, in the praise of the God who gave Christ for us.

context.literary: This is the climax and conclusion of the two-chapter appeal for the collection. Paul moves from the practical delegation of 8:16 to 9:5 into the principles that govern the giving heart, then rises to a doxology in verse 15. The passage is knit together by the imagery of sowing, seed, and harvest, and by the repeated theme of grace overflowing into thanksgiving.
context.historical: Agricultural imagery was immediately intelligible to Paul's readers, for whom sowing and reaping framed the rhythm of life and the ever-present risk of famine. The collection was destined for the impoverished believers in Jerusalem, and Paul foresees that the relief would kindle prayer and thanksgiving among them for their Gentile benefactors, strengthening the bond between the two halves of the church.
context.canonical: Paul draws on the Old Testament wisdom and psalm tradition, quoting Psalm 112:9 in verse 9 and echoing the manna and harvest themes of the Torah. The chapter has become the classic New Testament text on the spirit of Christian giving, and verse 15 stands as one of Scripture's briefest and richest doxologies.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To move the Corinthians to give generously and gladly by setting their giving within the promises and character of God, assuring them of His all-sufficient supply, and lifting their eyes from the gift to the Giver, so that their generosity ends in thanksgiving and the glory of God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The principle that God loves a cheerful giver and the call to give as one has decided in the heart are prescriptive for all believers. The promises of God supplying and multiplying are descriptive of God's faithful character, to be trusted, not treated as a mechanical guarantee of personal wealth.

debates.question: Does sow generously, reap generously promise material wealth to the giver, as prosperity teaching claims?

views.label: A misreading corrected by the context
views.case: The historic reading holds that the harvest Paul promises is God's all-sufficient grace, a multiplied harvest of righteousness, and abundant thanksgiving to God, not guaranteed personal riches; God supplies so that we may abound in every good work, not so that we may accumulate.

views.label: A promise of material return
views.case: Prosperity teaching takes the sowing and reaping language to promise financial increase to the generous, reading the enrichment of verse 11 as personal wealth.

debates.bearing: The context decisively favors the historic reading: the enrichment of verse 11 is explicitly so that you can be generous on every occasion and results in thanksgiving to God, making the reaping a harvest of righteousness and worship rather than a scheme for getting rich.

debates.question: What is the indescribable gift of verse 15?

views.label: Christ Himself, the ground of all grace
views.case: The most natural and widely held reading takes the indescribable gift as Jesus Christ, God's own Son, the gift so great that no words can compass it and the source of every grace named in the chapter.

views.label: The whole grace of God at work in the collection
views.case: Some see the gift as the entire outworking of grace Paul has described, the generosity, unity, and thanksgiving flowing among the churches, which so exceeds words that Paul can only praise God for it.

debates.bearing: The readings are not opposed; the grace at work in the collection flows from Christ, so that even those who read the gift more broadly find its fountain in the Son, and the doxology finally lands on God's supreme gift of Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The passage turns on three words. First, hilaros, cheerful, the root of our word hilarious, describing the glad-hearted giver whom God loves, one who gives not from regret or compulsion but from joy. Second, perisseuo, to abound or overflow, which Paul uses for the grace God makes to abound so that His people overflow in every good work, a cup filled past the brim. Third, eucharistia, thanksgiving, the goal toward which the whole ministry moves, for generosity meeting need produces many expressions of thanks to God. Together they trace the circuit of grace: God gives so that we give cheerfully and abundantly, and our giving returns to Him as worship, climaxing in thanks for His indescribable gift.

typology.type: The God who supplies seed to the sower and bread for food
typology.fulfillment: The Creator who provides the harvest points to the God of all grace in Christ, who supplies both the material seed and the spiritual increase, the harvest of righteousness.
God's gift of His own Son, Jesus Christ, is the fountain of every grace named in the chapter and the true harvest toward which all giving and thanksgiving finally point.

study.christ: Though Christ is not named in every verse, He is the sum of the whole passage. The saints glorify God for the obedient confession of the gospel of Christ, and the closing doxology thanks God for His indescribable gift, which the church has always understood first and foremost as Christ Himself, the Son given for our salvation. Christian generosity is a response to that supreme gift and a faint imitation of it; the God who did not withhold His own Son teaches His people to give freely and cheerfully. Every harvest of righteousness in the giver is finally the fruit of Christ's own self-giving.
study.god: This passage is saturated with God. He loves the cheerful giver, so that the heart behind the gift matters supremely to Him. He is able to make all grace abound, supplying every need at all times. He gives seed to the sower and multiplies it, increasing the harvest of righteousness. He is the one to whom thanksgiving rises when needs are met, and He is the Giver of the indescribable gift. The chapter shows a God whose generosity is the source, sustainer, and goal of all human giving, so that He is glorified from beginning to end.

crossReferences.note: He has distributed freely, he has given to the poor, his righteousness endures forever, quoted in verse 9.
One gives freely, yet grows all the richer; whoever brings blessing will be enriched.
My God will supply every need of yours according to His riches in glory in Christ Jesus.
God so loved the world that He gave His only Son, the indescribable gift behind all Christian generosity.

study.oneStory: Scripture unfolds the story of a giving God whose generosity climaxes in the gift of His Son. The cheerful giver is a child of that God, sowing freely because he has received the indescribable gift, and trusting the One who supplies and multiplies the seed. And the whole movement of grace returns to its source, for generosity meeting need overflows in thanksgiving, so that human giving ends where it began, in the praise of the God who gave Christ for us.

quiz.q: What kind of giver does God love, according to verse 7?

options.text: A cheerful giver, who gives from a glad heart, not from regret or compulsion.
options.note: God loves the hilaros, cheerful, giver whose generosity springs from joy.

options.text: A giver who gives the largest possible amount.
options.note: The heart behind the gift, not its size, is Paul's point.

options.text: A giver who gives only when he feels obligated.
options.note: Paul excludes giving out of compulsion; God loves cheerful giving.

options.text: A giver who lets everyone see how much he gives.
options.note: The concern is the gladness of the heart, not public display.

quiz.q: Why does God make all grace abound to His people?

options.text: So that, having all they need, they may abound in every good work.
options.note: Verse 8 says the abundance is for overflowing in good works, not hoarding.

options.text: So that they may become personally wealthy.
options.note: The aim is generosity and good works, not personal riches.

options.text: So that they will never face any hardship.
options.note: God supplies sufficiency for good works, not a life free of hardship.

options.text: So that they can stop depending on Him.
options.note: His supply deepens dependence and generosity, not independence.

quiz.q: What does the Corinthians' generosity ultimately produce?

options.text: Thanksgiving and glory to God from those whose needs are met.
options.note: Verses 11 to 13 show generosity overflowing in thanksgiving to God.

options.text: A reputation that makes the Corinthians famous.
options.note: The result is praise to God, not fame for the givers.

options.text: A debt that the recipients must repay.
options.note: It produces prayer and thanks, not obligation to repay.

options.text: Nothing beyond the meeting of physical needs.
options.note: It does more than meet needs; it rises to God as worship.

quiz.q: What is the indescribable gift Paul praises God for in verse 15?

options.text: Christ Himself, God's own Son, the fountain of every grace.
options.note: The doxology lands on God's supreme gift of Christ.

options.text: The money collected for the Jerusalem church.
options.note: The collection flows from the gift, but the gift itself is far greater.

options.text: The eloquence God gave Paul to write the letter.
options.note: The gift is God's own, not Paul's ability.

options.text: A special reward promised to generous givers.
options.note: The gift is Christ, given to all, not a payment for giving.

questions: God loves a cheerful giver, not merely a giver. How does the state of your heart, and not just the size of your gift, matter to God?
Paul roots giving in the promise that God is able to make all grace abound. Where do fears about not having enough keep you from generosity, and how does this promise speak to them?
The passage warns against giving out of regret or compulsion. What would need to change for your giving to become glad and free rather than pressured?
Generosity meeting need overflows in thanksgiving to God. How does it reframe your giving to see it as producing worship, not just transferring resources?
The chapter ends with thanks for God's indescribable gift in Christ. How does dwelling on the gift you have received in Jesus fuel your own generosity?

translationNotes.note: Cheerful renders hilaros, the source of the English word hilarious, denoting a glad, joyous readiness; the clause echoes the Septuagint addition at Proverbs 22:8.
The quotation is from Psalm 112:9; set as poetry in the source, it is rendered here as prose within the verse, with quotation marks retained.
Indescribable renders a word meaning inexpressible or beyond words; the gift is most naturally understood as Christ Himself, the source of every grace named in the chapter.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Corinthians 9:6-15. No em dashes required substitution in this passage. Footnote markers were removed. The Psalm 112:9 quotation in verse 9, set as poetry in the source, is rendered as prose within the verse with quotation marks retained. Sermon set to none; no expositor URL was verified this session.
